perturbation.original_text: All information disclosed by one party to the other to evaluate the Project UW Oshkosh Revised 12/16/19 and/or Purpose that is designated in writing as “Confidential” at the time of disclosure or if disclosed orally is designated in writing as “Confidential” within fifteen (15) days of disclosure is “Confidential Information.”
perturbation.changed_text: All information disclosed by one party to the other to evaluate the Project UW Oshkosh Revised 12/16/19 and/or Purpose that is designated in writing as “Confidential” at the time of disclosure or if disclosed orally is designated in writing as “Confidential” within thirty (30) days of disclosure is “Confidential Information.”
perturbation.explanation: The contract initially requires oral disclosures to be designated as confidential in writing within 15 days. Changing this to 30 days could potentially create inconsistencies with Wisconsin's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Delaying the written designation might be seen as a failure to take reasonable measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: WUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Designating orally disclosed information as confidential in writing is a method to maintain secrecy. Extending the deadline for written designation to 30 days may be argued as not being 'reasonable' in certain contexts.

perturbation.justification: The change from 15 to 30 days for designating orally disclosed information as confidential in writing potentially contradicts the requirement to make reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy under WUTSA, as noted in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address trade secrets, the Perturbation and Law Explanations strongly suggest that the extended timeframe could be viewed as unreasonable and thus non-compliant with the reasonable efforts standard of WUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: To the extent allowed by the law applicable to the Receiving Party, including, but not limited to, for UW -Oshkosh, Wis. Stat. §§ 893.82 & 895.46,” the Receiving Party will be liable for any breach of this agreement by any of its employees, agents, affiliates or representatives that receive access to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will not be liable for any breach of this agreement by any of its employees, agents, affiliates or representatives that receive access to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the liability clause for breaches by employees contradicts the principle of vicarious liability, which, while limited, can hold employers responsible for employee actions in certain cases under Wisconsin law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Statute 895.46 provides for the state to pay judgments taken against state officers or employees. While it doesn't mandate liability in all cases, eliminating the possibility of liability contradicts the intent of the statute, particularly when employee actions are within the scope of their employment and relate to confidential information covered by the NDA.
perturbation.justification: The original text, within legal limits, made the Receiving Party liable for breaches caused by its personnel. The changed text removes all liability for these breaches. The Perturbation Explanation states this removal contradicts vicarious liability principles under Wisconsin law, which can hold employers responsible for employee actions. The Law Explanation further clarifies that while Wisconsin Statute 895.46 doesn't mandate liability in all cases, completely eliminating the possibility of liability contradicts the statute's intent, especially when employee actions fall within their job scope and involve confidential information. Therefore, by eliminating liability altogether, the changed text creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party’s designated representative may maintain one copy of all Confidential Information for the purpose of addressing any claim that may be brought under this Agreement and to comply wit h any other legal or recordkeeping requirements, and neither party will be obligated to destroy any Confidential Information that is stored electronically on back -up systems or computer hard drives after a file is deleted, but any such electronic informati on will continue to be subject to the terms of confidentiality under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party’s designated representative may maintain one copy of all Confidential Information for the purpose of addressing any claim that may be brought under this Agreement and to comply wit h any other legal or recordkeeping requirements, and neither party will be obligated to destroy any Confidential Information that is stored electronically on back -up systems or computer hard drives after a file is deleted, and such electronic information will not be subject to the terms of confidentiality under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that electronic information stored on back-up systems or computer hard drives is not subject to the confidentiality terms, this directly contradicts the Wisconsin Uniform Trade Secrets Act (WUTSA), which requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, and would likely mean the receiving party is not taking reasonable steps to maintain the secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: WUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Exempting electronic information on backups from confidentiality obligations would be considered a failure to take reasonable steps and would undermine trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The original text states that electronic information will continue to be subject to the terms of confidentiality. The changed text explicitly states that such electronic information will not be subject to the terms of confidentiality, directly contradicting the original agreement. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this undermines trade secret protection under WUTSA because it fails to take reasonable steps to maintain secrecy. Although no specific snippet from WUTSA is provided, the consistent explanation of the legal requirement to maintain secrecy and the direct contradiction in the changed text lead to the conclusion that a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement shall begin on the date of the last signature on this Agreement and expire after one (1) year, unless terminated earlier by a party with thirty (30) days prior written notice. The obligations and restrictions of confidentiality of the Receiving Party under this Agreement shall continue for a period of three (3) years from the date of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement shall begin on the date of the last signature on this Agreement and expire after one (1) year, unless terminated earlier by a party with one (1) day prior written notice. The obligations and restrictions of confidentiality of the Receiving Party under this Agreement shall continue for a period of three (3) years from the date of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination notice period from 30 days to 1 day may be deemed unreasonable and unconscionable, violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing inherent in Wisconsin contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Wisconsin Contract Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin contract law includes an implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.  While not a statute, Wisconsin Jury Instruction - Civil 3044 explains the concept. A one-day notice period for termination could be considered a breach of this duty if it demonstrably frustrates the other party's reasonable expectations and prevents them from benefiting from the agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text provides a 30-day notice period for termination, while the changed text reduces this to a 1-day notice. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that this change could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Wisconsin contract law. Although there are no Scraped Law Snippets to directly support this, the explanations provided highlight that the reduced notice period may be deemed unreasonable and frustrate the other party's reasonable expectations, which would contradict the implied duty of good faith.
